questions.question: A money transfer debits one account and credits another. The system crashes after the debit but before the credit. What guarantee prevents money from vanishing?
options: Atomicity — the transaction is all-or-nothing, so the debit is rolled back
questions.explanation: Atomicity makes a transaction all-or-nothing. If the credit never happens, the debit is undone on recovery, so the database never ends up in the half-completed state where money disappeared. Both updates commit together or neither does.

questions.question: Why don't databases just always use the strongest isolation level?
options: Stronger isolation requires more locking/coordination, which reduces concurrency and throughput
It is illegal in some databases
Weaker levels are always more correct
questions.explanation: Perfect isolation (Serializable) makes transactions behave as if run one at a time, which requires locking or extra coordination that limits how many can run concurrently. So databases offer a dial; you pick the weakest level still correct for your use case.

questions.question: What is a 'lost update'?
options: Two transactions both read the same value, both modify it, and both write back — so one update is silently overwritten
A query that returns no rows
questions.explanation: If T1 and T2 both read X=10, both compute 11, and both write 11, the result is 11 when it should be 12 — one update is lost. The fix is an atomic 'SET x = x + 1', a higher isolation level, or explicit locking (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE).

questions.question: Which isolation level makes transactions behave exactly as if they ran one at a time?
questions.explanation: Serializable is the strongest level: it prevents dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, and phantoms, making concurrent execution equivalent to some serial order. It's the most correct but the slowest. Read Committed is a common high-concurrency default.

questions.question: How does MVCC improve concurrency compared to pure locking?
options: It deletes old data to free locks
It keeps multiple versions of a row so readers see a consistent snapshot without blocking writers (and vice versa)
It forces all transactions to run serially
questions.explanation: Multi-Version Concurrency Control stores several versions of each row. A reader sees a consistent snapshot as of when its transaction started, so it doesn't block writers and writers don't block it — far better concurrency than locking every row a reader touches.
